name: Iceland

outbound_official.note: No official Iceland government outbound visa-list page located. Iceland's Ministry for Foreign Affairs (government.is) and the island.is portal do not publish a static country-by-country list of where Icelandic ordinary-passport holders can travel without a prior consular visa. All government.is pages returned HTTP 402; exhaustive search of island.is, government.is, and utl.is found only inbound visa pages (for foreign nationals coming to Iceland). Unlike India's MEA (mea.gov.in/vffin), Iceland has no equivalent origin-side outbound travel authorization page. Rely on destination-side data.

visa_free.nationality: All EU member state nationals (EEA freedom of movement)
visa_free.notes: EU/EEA citizens have unlimited freedom of movement to live, work and reside in Iceland under the EEA Agreement. No visa required. May use national ID card in lieu of passport.
Fellow EFTA/EEA states. Citizens of Norway and Liechtenstein enjoy freedom of movement to Iceland under the EEA Agreement with no stay limit.
Switzerland participates in Schengen and has a bilateral agreement with the EU/EEA. Swiss nationals have free movement rights and no visa requirement for Iceland.

visa_free.nationality: Nordic countries (Denmark, Sweden, Finland, Norway) – Nordic Passport Union
visa_free.notes: Iceland is a founding member of the Nordic Passport Union (since 1 January 1966). Nordic citizens (Denmark, Finland, Norway, Sweden) may travel to Iceland without any identity documentation, with no stay limit.

Biometric passport required - non-biometric passport holders require a Schengen visa. Schengen Annex II visa-exempt (EU Regulation 2018/1806). 90 days in any 180-day period. ETIAS pre-travel authorization will also be required once ETIAS launches (expected Q4 2026, not yet in force as of June 2026).

Schengen Annex II visa-exempt (EU Regulation 2018/1806, Annex II). Monaco has open borders with France (a Schengen member) and applies de facto Schengen visa rules. 90 days in any 180-day period. Monaco nationals are also exempt from ETIAS.

Schengen Annex II visa-exempt (EU Regulation 2018/1806, Annex II). San Marino has open borders with Italy (a Schengen member) and applies de facto Schengen visa rules. 90 days in any 180-day period. San Marino nationals are also exempt from ETIAS.

Schengen Annex II visa-exempt (EU Regulation 2018/1806, Annex II). The Holy See has open borders with Italy (a Schengen member) and applies de facto Schengen visa rules. 90 days in any 180-day period. Holy See nationals are also exempt from ETIAS.

Biometric passport required (issued within previous 10 years, valid at least 3 months beyond intended departure). Kosovo transferred from Annex I to Annex II via Regulation (EU) 2023/850; visa-free regime effective 1 January 2024. Applies to Iceland as an associated Schengen state. 90 days in any 180-day period. ETIAS pre-travel authorization will also be required once ETIAS launches (expected Q4 2026, not yet in force as of June 2026). Note: XKX is a user-assigned code; Kosovo has no ISO 3166-1 standardised code.

visa_policy.notes: Iceland is a full Schengen Area member and an EEA/EFTA state. It applies the Schengen Common Visa Policy (EU Regulation 2018/1806). There is no Iceland-specific e-visa or visa-on-arrival scheme; a Schengen short-stay visa issued by any Schengen state is valid for Iceland. The official visa-checker tool at island.is/en/do-you-need-a-visa does not publish a static country list - it uses an interactive tool. Full Annex II country enumeration is based on EU Regulation 2018/1806 as applicable to Iceland per its Schengen association agreement. Vanuatu was removed from Annex II in December 2024 via Regulation (EU) 2025/11 and now requires a visa. Kosovo was added to Annex II by Regulation (EU) 2023/850, effective 1 January 2024 (biometric passport required). The EU Entry/Exit System (EES) has been operational at Iceland's borders since 10 April 2026, requiring biometric registration of non-EEA/Schengen travelers on arrival.

credential.subtype: any valid Schengen visa (uniform visa or national long-stay visa)

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a valid Schengen visa (uniform short-stay visa or national long-stay visa) issued by any Schengen member state do not need to apply for a separate Iceland visa. Stay subject to the 90/180-day Schengen rule. Visa must be valid.

credential.subtype: any valid residence permit issued by an EEA/EFTA country

conditional_access.conditions: Non-EEA/EFTA nationals who hold a valid residence permit issued by another EEA/EFTA country may enter Iceland without a separate Iceland visa, for up to 90 days in any 180-day period.

credential.subtype: EU family member residence card issued by any EU member state

conditional_access.conditions: Residence cards for family members of EU/EEA nationals issued by any EU member state qualify for entry to Iceland without a separate visa.

Nationals of Australia, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Israel, Japan, Malaysia, Mexico, New Zealand, South Korea, and Uruguay may stay in Iceland for up to 3 months beyond the standard 90-day Schengen allowance, for a total of approximately 180 days, under bilateral extended-stay agreements. Applies to bona fide non-immigrants only. Right is forfeited if a residence permit application is submitted during the stay. Cannot apply for a residence permit while in Iceland under this agreement.
Holders of diplomatic passports, special passports, and service passports issued by Turkey are exempt from the visa requirement for Iceland.
Holders of diplomatic passports and service passports issued by India are exempt from the visa requirement for Iceland. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of diplomatic passports and service passports issued by Pakistan are exempt from the visa requirement for Iceland. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of diplomatic passports and service passports issued by South Africa are exempt from the visa requirement for Iceland. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of diplomatic passports issued by China are exempt from the visa requirement for Iceland. Service and ordinary passport holders require a visa.

cbi.notes: Iceland has no citizenship-by-investment (CBI) program. Icelandic citizenship is only acquired by birth, descent, adoption, or naturalisation through residence. General naturalisation requires 7 years of continuous domicile in Iceland. Shorter periods apply for: spouses married to an Icelandic citizen (4 years since marriage, Icelandic spouse must have been citizen for 5+ years), cohabiting partners of Icelandic citizens (5 years), Nordic citizens (4 years), children of Icelandic citizens (2 years), refugees/humanitarian permit holders (5 years), stateless persons (5 years). Dual citizenship is permitted since the 1 July 2003 amendment to the Nationality Act. Icelandic language proficiency is required for most paths. There is no investment or donation pathway to citizenship. Sources: island.is/en/electronic-application-for-icelandic-citizenship/when-can-I-apply, government.is/topics/foreign-nationals/citizenship/.

programs.name: Residence Permit for Qualified Professionals (Specialist Employees)
programs.notes: For specialist/expert employees. Requires an employment contract meeting statutory minimum wage, valid health insurance (min 6 months, ISK 2,000,000 coverage), criminal record certificate, and valid passport. Initial permit for 1 year, renewable for 2 years. Expedited processing available for qualified professionals (within 30 days if all documents submitted; standard is up to 180 days). Permanent residence possible after 4 continuous years. No investment threshold.

programs.name: Residence Permit for Employees (Labour Shortage)
programs.notes: For positions in labour shortage occupations. Same general requirements as specialist employee permit. No investment pathway.

programs.name: Residence Permit on Grounds of Legitimate and Special Purpose
programs.notes: Covers specific personal circumstances: injury/illness requiring extended stay, being a relative of injured/ill person, expecting a child with Icelandic citizenship, or party to an Icelandic court case. Requires secure means of support: ISK 259,951/month (individual), ISK 415,922/month (couple), plus ISK 129,976 per additional adult family member. Not an investment or golden visa program.

programs.name: Expedited Processing for Qualified Professionals
programs.eligibility: Non-EEA nationals hired by Icelandic employers for specialist/expert roles requiring university-level or technical qualifications. Employer must demonstrate the role is necessary and the applicant is the best fit.
programs.notes: An expedited application for qualified professionals (specialist employees) is processed within 30 days provided all supporting documents have been submitted. The expedited option currently applies only to the main applicant and not to family members. Official program on work.iceland.is, operated by the Directorate of Immigration.

programs.name: Long-Term Visa for Remote Workers
programs.eligibility: Non-EEA nationals who are visa-exempt to enter Schengen and have not received an Icelandic long-term visa in the past 12 months. Must work remotely for a foreign employer or be self-employed abroad. Monthly income must be at least ISK 1,000,000 (ISK 1,300,000 if applying with spouse/partner or dependent children under 18). Must hold valid international health insurance.
programs.processing_time: 3–4 weeks from receipt of complete application
programs.notes: Iceland's Long-Term Visa for Remote Work (application form L-802). Allows up to 180 days in Iceland for remote workers employed by foreign companies or self-employed abroad. Applicants from outside Schengen receive full 180-day validity; applicants already in Schengen may receive up to 90 days. Application fee: ISK 12,200 (~USD 88). Only issued once per 12-month period. Applications submitted by post to Directorate of Immigration, Dalvegur 18, 201 Kópavogur. Not a residence permit - holder cannot obtain an Icelandic ID number. Source corroborated by work.iceland.is (official Promote Iceland / government portal).

visa_types.name: Schengen Short-Stay Visa (Type C)
visa_types.purpose: For tourism, visiting family or friends, business trips, and other short stays in Iceland and the Schengen Area. Covers any lawful purpose up to 90 days.
visa_types.notes: Fee is EUR 90 (approximately USD 97); EUR 45 for children aged 6–12; free for children under 6. Iceland is a Schengen member - a Schengen C visa issued by any Schengen state is valid for Iceland. Visa-exempt nationals do not need this visa. As of May 13 2026 visa stamp issuance was transferred from the Directorate of Immigration to the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Applications directed to the appropriate Icelandic embassy or consulate. Processing approximately two weeks when documents are complete.

visa_types.name: Visa Extension (Short-Stay)
visa_types.purpose: Extension of a Schengen short-stay visa already issued for less than 90 days, due to force majeure, humanitarian circumstances, or serious personal reasons.
visa_types.notes: Only visas with less than 90 days validity are eligible for extension. Extensions cannot push total stay beyond 90 days in a 180-day period. Free for force majeure/humanitarian grounds; ISK 4,600 for serious personal reasons. Applications submitted electronically to visa@utn.is. Required documents: passport copy, current visa copy, travel medical insurance, explanatory letter, and medical certificate if applicable.

visa_types.name: Bilateral Extended Stay (Tourist)
visa_types.purpose: Allows nationals of specific countries with bilateral agreements to stay in Iceland for up to 3 additional months beyond the standard 90-day Schengen allowance, for tourism purposes only.
visa_types.notes: Available to citizens of: Australia, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Israel, Japan, Malaysia, Mexico, New Zealand, South Korea, and Uruguay. Applies only to bona fide non-immigrants (tourists). The right to stay is forfeited if a residence permit application is submitted while in Iceland under this agreement. Cannot apply for a residence permit while in Iceland under this arrangement. No formal visa - entry right under bilateral treaty.

visa_types.name: Residence Permit Based on Work (Expert/Specialist)
visa_types.purpose: For non-EEA/EFTA nationals hired by Icelandic employers in positions requiring expert knowledge, specialist skills, or university-level qualifications.
visa_types.notes: Fee: ISK 80,000 (non-refundable). Application fee paid by bank transfer; paper application submitted to Directorate of Immigration, Dalvegur 18, Kópavogur, or District Commissioner offices. Covers expert knowledge, labour shortage, athletic employment, and collaboration/service contracts under Articles 61–64 of the Foreign Nationals Act. Expedited processing within 30 days for qualified professionals when documents are complete; standard processing up to 180 days. Initial permit typically 1 year, renewable for 2 years. Visa-required nationals cannot be in Iceland during processing for labour shortage applications; expert/athlete applicants may remain if legally present. Employer must obtain work permit from Directorate of Labour before residence permit is issued.

visa_types.name: Working Holiday Residence Permit
visa_types.purpose: Allows young adults from eligible countries to work and holiday in Iceland for an extended period beyond standard tourist stay limits.
visa_types.notes: Eligible countries: Andorra, Canada, Chile, Japan, United Kingdom. Fee: ISK 40,000 for applicants from Andorra, Canada, Chile, and the UK; no fee for Japanese citizens. Fees are non-refundable if applicant cancels. Applications submitted on paper by mail to the Directorate of Immigration or District Commissioner offices. Fee must be paid by bank transfer before submission. Eligible nationals do not require a visa and may remain in Iceland while the application is processed, provided their Schengen Area stay does not exceed 90 days in 180 days. Specific permit duration not published on the official page.

visa_types.name: Long-Term Visa for Remote Workers
visa_types.purpose: Allows non-EEA nationals who work remotely for a foreign employer or are self-employed abroad to live and work in Iceland for up to 180 days.
visa_types.notes: Application form L-802. Only available to visa-exempt nationals entering from outside the Schengen Area (those already in Schengen may receive reduced validity up to 90 days). Monthly income requirement: ISK 1,000,000 (individual); ISK 1,300,000 (with spouse/partner or dependent children under 18). Must hold valid international health insurance. Fee: ISK 12,200 (~USD 88). Only issued once per 12-month period. Not a residence permit - holder cannot obtain an Icelandic ID number. Applications by post to Directorate of Immigration. Processing 3–4 weeks from receipt of complete application.

visa_types.name: Residence Permit for Students
visa_types.purpose: For non-EEA/EFTA nationals enrolled in full-time studies at recognized Icelandic universities, postgraduate doctoral programs, approved exchange programs, internships integrated with studies, or recognized secondary-level technical training.
visa_types.notes: Fee: ISK 70,000 (non-refundable), paid by bank transfer before submission. Applications must be received by May 1 for the fall semester or October 1 for the spring semester. Required documents: valid passport, health insurance confirmation, proof of secure financial support, criminal record certificate, and admission letter from recognized Icelandic institution. Visa-required applicants cannot be in Iceland during processing; visa-exempt applicants may remain if their Schengen stay is within 90/180-day limits. Applications submitted by mail or drop box at Directorate of Immigration, Kópavogur.

visa_types.name: Residence Permit for Spouse/Cohabiting Partner
visa_types.purpose: For non-EEA/EFTA nationals moving to Iceland to live with a spouse or cohabiting partner who has the right to reside in Iceland.
visa_types.notes: Fee: ISK 110,000 (non-refundable), paid by bank transfer before submission. Covers both marriage and cohabitation arrangements. If the sponsor is an EEA/EFTA citizen, applicant may instead apply for a family member residence card. Visa-exempt applicants may generally stay while the application is processed; visa-required applicants must typically obtain a permit before entering Iceland. If the sponsor is a student or on a service agreement, applicant generally cannot stay beyond 90-day visa-free period without compelling fairness reasons. Granted under Article 70 of Iceland's Foreign Nationals Act.

visa_types.name: Residence Permit for Children (Family Reunification)
visa_types.purpose: For children under 18 to be reunited with their parents who have the right to reside in Iceland.
visa_types.notes: Fee: ISK 60,000 (non-refundable), paid by bank transfer before submission. Child must be under 18 and in custody of the parent seeking reunification. Parents with permanent residence permits may also apply for children born after obtaining their own permit. Applications submitted by mail or in person to the Directorate of Immigration, Kópavogur. Processing time not specified; current wait times available on Directorate's waiting time page.

visa_types.name: Residence Permit for Parents (aged 67+)
visa_types.purpose: For parents aged 67 or older to join their children who are legally residing in Iceland.
visa_types.notes: Available to parents aged 67 or older of persons with right of residence in Iceland. Paper application to Directorate of Immigration. Fee not confirmed from direct page fetch but consistent with other family permit structures (typically ISK 60,000–110,000). Part of Iceland's family reunification regime under the Foreign Nationals Act.

visa_types.name: Au Pair Residence Permit
visa_types.purpose: For non-EEA/EFTA nationals aged 18–25 to live with an Icelandic host family and provide childcare and light household work in exchange for pocket money, as a cultural exchange arrangement.
visa_types.notes: Fee: ISK 120,000 (non-refundable), paid by bank transfer before submission. Applicants must be aged 18–25 and non-EEA/EFTA citizens. Visa-required applicants cannot be in Iceland during application or processing (rare exceptions apply). Visa-exempt applicants must maintain legal stay under 90/180-day Schengen limits. Host family must have no familial connection with the au pair. Applications submitted to Directorate of Immigration or District Commissioner offices.

visa_types.name: Residence Permit for Volunteers
visa_types.purpose: For non-EEA/EFTA nationals participating in approved voluntary service programs in Iceland.
visa_types.notes: For participants in recognized volunteer programs in Iceland. Paper application to Directorate of Immigration. Specific fee, duration, and processing time not obtained from direct page fetch. Part of Iceland's special-purpose residence permit regime.

visa_types.name: Residence Permit for Legitimate and Special Purpose
visa_types.purpose: A discretionary permit for exceptional circumstances where applicants do not meet requirements for other permit categories, such as extended illness requiring stay, relatives of ill persons, expectant mothers of children with Icelandic citizenship, or parties to Icelandic court proceedings.
visa_types.notes: Fee: ISK 40,000 (non-refundable). Granted only in exceptional cases under Article 79 of the Foreign Nationals Act. Does not create an obligation to grant the permit - decision is based on overall assessment. Financial self-sufficiency required: ISK 259,951/month (individual), ISK 415,922/month (couple). Visa-required applicants cannot be in Iceland during processing except for compelling fairness reasons. Visa-exempt applicants may remain within legal Schengen limits.

visa_types.name: International Protection (Refugee / Subsidiary / Humanitarian)
visa_types.purpose: Protection-based residence status for persons who qualify as refugees, or who face serious harm or humanitarian circumstances preventing return to their country of origin.
visa_types.notes: Covers three sub-categories: refugee status (Convention refugee), subsidiary protection (serious harm risk), and humanitarian permit (individual or collective). Applications submitted at the border or within Iceland to the Directorate of Immigration. No application fee for protection claims. Approved applicants receive residence permits and may access Directorate of Labour services. Family members of recognized refugees may apply for protection-based family reunification (spouse, children, parents, siblings of refugee children).

agreements: EEA freedom of movement (European Economic Area)
Bilateral extended-stay agreements (Australia, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Israel, Japan, Malaysia, Mexico, New Zealand, South Korea, Uruguay)

validation_notes: CORRECTION: Vanuatu removed from Annex II visa-free list. Vanuatu was transferred from Schengen Annex II (visa-free) to Annex I (visa required) in December 2024 via Regulation (EU) 2025/11, due to its investor citizenship scheme enabling visa-required third-country nationals to bypass Schengen requirements. Vanuatu national reference removed from the visa-free notes.

CORRECTION: CBI notes updated to fix spousal naturalization path. Prior record stated '3 years for spouses of Icelandic citizens'; official island.is page confirms it is 4 years (married to Icelandic citizen, since marriage) and 5 years for cohabiting partners. The 3-year figure was incorrect.

ADDITION: Bilateral extended-stay agreements added to conditional_access. Eleven countries (Australia, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Israel, Japan, Malaysia, Mexico, New Zealand, South Korea, Uruguay) have bilateral agreements allowing up to 3 additional months (total ~180 days) beyond the standard 90-day Schengen allowance. This was in sources_checked but not captured as a conditional_access entry; now correctly classified as basis='bilateral_agreement'.

ADDITION: CBI notes updated to include full naturalization table (spouse 4yr, cohabitant 5yr, child 2yr, Nordic citizen 4yr, refugee 5yr, stateless 5yr) verified from island.is/en/electronic-application-for-icelandic-citizenship/when-can-I-apply.
CORRECTION: ETIAS fee corrected. Prior record stated €20; the authoritative ETIAS page indicates the fee is €7 (for applicants over 18). This note flagged for verification at time of travel.

UPDATE: ETIAS/EES timeline updated. The EU Entry/Exit System (EES) became fully operational at Iceland's borders on 10 April 2026 (phased rollout began 12 October 2025). ETIAS expected Q4 2026 but mandatory enforcement may begin ~6 months later (circa April 2027). ETIAS source_url updated to revised-timeline page.
ADDITION: agreements[] updated to include bilateral extended-stay agreement countries.
VERIFIED: No CBI program - confirmed no investment/donation pathway to Icelandic citizenship on official sources.
VERIFIED: No visa-on-arrival or e-visa for Iceland - correct, Iceland uses Schengen common visa system only.
VERIFIED: Permanent residence after 4 continuous years - confirmed via island.is/en/permanent-residence-permit/requirements.
VERIFIED: General naturalization at 7 years - confirmed via island.is/en/electronic-application-for-icelandic-citizenship/when-can-I-apply.

2026-06-27 ADDITION: Monaco (MCO), San Marino (SMR), and Holy See/Vatican City (VAT) added to visa_free list. These three European microstates are listed in Annex II of Regulation (EU) 2018/1806 without any footnotes or conditions. They have open borders with adjacent Schengen member states (France and Italy) and apply de facto Schengen visa rules. They were omitted from the prior record. Source: eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32018R1806.

2026-06-27 VERIFIED: Nicaragua (NIC) remains in Schengen Annex II (visa-free for Schengen entry including Iceland). Nicaragua imposed its own visa requirements on nationals of Iceland and certain EU states effective February 2026 (Decree 002-2026), but the EU has NOT suspended Nicaragua from Annex II. Nicaragua remains visa-free to enter Iceland.

2026-06-27 VERIFIED: Georgia (GEO) ordinary passport holders with biometric passports remain visa-free for Iceland under Annex II. The EU suspended visa-free access for Georgian DIPLOMATIC/SERVICE/OFFICIAL passport holders from March 6, 2026 to March 6, 2027 (not ordinary passports). Existing record correctly shows Georgia as visa_free (ordinary, biometric required). The diplomatic exemption in conditional_access should note this suspension.

2026-06-27 NOT ADDED: Fiji (FJI) and Nauru (NRU) are listed in Annex II of Regulation (EU) 2018/1806 with footnote 6, meaning the exemption only applies from the date of entry into force of an EU visa exemption agreement - no such agreement is currently in force. Fiji and Nauru ordinary passport holders therefore require a Schengen visa to enter Iceland. Not added to visa_free.

2026-06-27 VERIFIED: Bilateral extended-stay agreements confirmed via island.is/en/entry-requirements-to-iceland/bilateral-agreements-on-extended-stay - all 11 countries (AUS, BRA, CAN, CHL, ISR, JPN, MYS, MEX, NZL, KOR, URY) confirmed.

2026-06-29 ADDITION: Kosovo (XKX) added to visa_free list. Kosovo was transferred from Annex I to Annex II by Regulation (EU) 2023/850, with the visa-free regime effective 1 January 2024. Applies to all associated Schengen states including Iceland. Biometric passport required. Confirmed via EEAS official FAQ page (eeas.europa.eu/delegations/kosovo/faqs-visa-free-travel-eu-schengen-area_en). This entry was missing from prior record despite being in effect since January 2024.
gaps: Iceland's official visa-checker at island.is/en/do-you-need-a-visa uses an interactive tool and does not publish a static country-by-country list; no official Iceland-specific static list of all visa-exempt nationalities was accessible.
The utl.is domain now redirects entirely to island.is; the legacy 'who-needs-a-visa' page is no longer accessible.
The government.is topic pages return HTTP 402 errors; full official policy text on citizenship and foreign nationals could not be directly scraped.

The Schengen Annex II visa-free country enumeration is derived from the EU regulatory framework (Regulation 2018/1806) applied to Iceland via the Schengen Association Agreement, not from a standalone Iceland government page.
Iceland has no golden visa or CBI program; confirmed via official-adjacent searches but no dedicated negative-confirmation page on a currently-accessible official government URL.

The work.iceland.is long-term visa page returned 404; program details confirmed via third-party summaries of official L-802 form requirements and work.iceland.is search results. Income thresholds (ISK 1,000,000 / ISK 1,300,000) corroborated by multiple secondary sources citing official figures but not directly verified from a live official page.
No official page enumerating all visa-required nationalities (Annex I) was accessible from Iceland-specific official sources.

Diplomatic/service passport exemptions for Turkey, India, Pakistan, South Africa, China confirmed from island.is interactive visa checker references but exact bilateral agreement dates and treaty names were not published on any accessible official page.
No official Icelandic government source found confirming OCI (Overseas Citizen of India) cardholder entry exemption; OCI not recorded.
The exact max_stay_days for diplomatic/service passport holders not explicitly stated on official pages; 90 days assumed as standard Schengen rule applies.

ETIAS fee: prior record stated €20; current information indicates €7 for adults over 18. The official ETIAS fee page was not accessible to directly confirm the current figure; flagged as unverified pending ETIAS launch.

Georgia diplomatic passport holders' Schengen visa-free access was suspended in March 2026 per EU decision (effective March 6, 2026 – March 6, 2027). As a full Schengen Area participant, Iceland applies the same suspension. The conditional_access entry for Georgia diplomatic/service passports should be treated as suspended until March 2027, but the exact bilateral scope has not been confirmed from an Iceland-specific official page.

Fiji (FJI) and Nauru (NRU) are listed in Annex II of Regulation (EU) 2018/1806 with footnote 6 (exemption only takes effect upon entry into force of an EU bilateral visa exemption agreement). No such agreement is confirmed in force as of June 2026; both countries therefore require a Schengen visa. Confirmed via EUR-Lex consolidated text and web search.

British nationals (Overseas), British overseas territories citizens (BOTC), British overseas citizens (BOC), British protected persons (BPP), and British subjects (BS) are listed separately in Annex II with their own exemptions. The current record only captures GBR (regular British citizens). These sub-categories cover very small populations with restricted travel documents; full delineation not captured in this record.
